[Warsaw metropolitan area](/wiki/Warsaw_metropolitan_area "Warsaw metropolitan area")

An **urban area**[[a]](#cite_note-2) is a [human settlement](/wiki/Human_settlement "Human settlement") with a high [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") and an [infrastructure](/wiki/Infrastructure "Infrastructure") of [built environment](/wiki/Built_environment "Built environment"). Urban areas originate through [urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization"), and researchers categorize them as cities, towns, [conurbations](/wiki/Conurbation "Conurbation") or [suburbs](/wiki/Suburbs "Suburbs"). In [urbanism](/wiki/Urbanism "Urbanism"), the term "urban area" contrasts to [rural areas](/wiki/Rural_area "Rural area") such as [villages](/wiki/Village "Village") and [hamlets](/wiki/Hamlet_(place) "Hamlet (place)"); in [urban sociology](/wiki/Urban_sociology "Urban sociology") or [urban anthropology](/wiki/Urban_anthropology "Urban anthropology"), it often contrasts with [natural environment](/wiki/Natural_environment "Natural environment").

The development of earlier predecessors of modern urban areas during the [urban revolution](/wiki/Urban_revolution "Urban revolution") of the [4th millennium BCE](/wiki/4th_millennium_BCE "4th millennium BCE")[[2]](#cite_note-3) led to the formation of human [civilization](/wiki/Civilization "Civilization") and ultimately to modern [urban planning](/wiki/Urban_planning "Urban planning"), which along with other human activities such as [exploitation of natural resources](/wiki/Exploitation_of_natural_resources "Exploitation of natural resources") has led to a [human impact on the environment](/wiki/Human_impact_on_the_environment "Human impact on the environment").

## Recent historical growth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Recent historical growth")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Global_land_use_for_food_production%2C_OWID_statistic.png/330px-Global_land_use_for_food_production%2C_OWID_statistic.png)](/wiki/File:Global_land_use_for_food_production,_OWID_statistic.png)

Earth's land use in 2019, built-up area being estimated as 1.5 million square kilometers

In 1950, 764 million people (or about 30 percent of the world's 2.5 billion people) lived in urban areas. In 2009, the number of people living in urban areas (3.42 billion) surpassed the number living in rural areas (3.41 billion), and since then the world has become more urban than rural.[[3]](#cite_note-4) By 2014, it was 3.9 billion (or about 53 percent of the world's 7.3 billion people) that lived in urban areas. The change was driven by a combination of increased total population and increased percent of population living in urban areas.[[4]](#cite_note-UrbanPopulation-5) This was the first time that the majority of the world's population lived in a city.[[5]](#cite_note-6) By that time a high estimate calculated up to 3.5 million square kilometers of land were urban, estimates ranging from 1% of global land area.[[6]](#cite_note-c171-7)[[7]](#cite_note-r019-8)

In 2014 there were 7.3 billion people living on the planet,[[8]](#cite_note-WorldPopulation-9) of which the global urban population comprised 3.9 billion. The Population Division of the [United Nations Department of Economic and Social Affairs](/wiki/United_Nations_Department_of_Economic_and_Social_Affairs "United Nations Department of Economic and Social Affairs") at that time predicted the urban population would occupy 68% of the world population by 2050, with 90% of that growth coming from [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") and [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia").[[9]](#cite_note-10)

## Urbanization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Urbanization")]

Main article: [Urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Cities_with_1M_People_in_2025.png/250px-Cities_with_1M_People_in_2025.png)](/wiki/File:Cities_with_1M_People_in_2025.png)

Urban areas with at least one million inhabitants in 2025

Urban areas are created and further developed by the process of urbanization. They are measured for various purposes, including analyzing [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") and [urban sprawl](/wiki/Urban_sprawl "Urban sprawl"). Urban areas are generally found in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"), [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"), [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), and many other countries where the urbanization rate is high.

Unlike an urban area, a [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area") includes not only the urban area, but also intervening rural land and [satellite cities](/wiki/Satellite_city "Satellite city") that are socio-economically connected to the urban area. The urban area serves as the core of a metropolitan area, typically by [employment](/wiki/Employment "Employment") ties through [commuting](/wiki/Commuting "Commuting"), with the urban area being the [primary labor market](/wiki/Primary_labor_market "Primary labor market").[[11]](#cite_note-12)

The concept of an "urban area" as used in economic statistics should not be confused with the concept of the "urban area" used in road safety statistics. This term was first created by Geographer Brian Manning. The last concept is also known as "[built-up area in road safety](/wiki/Built-up_area_(Highway_Code) "Built-up area (Highway Code)")". According to the definition by the [Office for National Statistics](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics"), "Built-up areas are defined as land which is 'irreversibly urban in character', meaning that they are characteristic of a town or city. They include areas of built-up land with a minimum of 20 hectares (200,000 m2; 49 acres). Any areas [separated by] less than 200 metres [of non-urban space] are linked to become a single built-up area.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

[Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") and [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") are countries where the urbanization rate is over 90% while [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile"), [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") are countries where the urbanization rate is between 80% and 90%, although within the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), the urbanization rate is 100%.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

There are two measures of the degree of urbanization of a population. The first, urban population, describes the percentage of the total population living in urban areas, as defined by the country. The second measure, rate of urbanization, describes the projected average rate of change of the size of the urban population over the given period of time. According to [Urbanization by sovereign state](/wiki/Urbanization_by_sovereign_state "Urbanization by sovereign state") article, the world as a whole is 56.2% urbanized, with roughly one-quarter of the countries reported as greater than 80% urbanized. ## Definitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Definitions")]

Presently, urban data are based on arbitrary definitions that vary from country to country and from year or census to the next, making them difficult to compare.

> The UN publishes data on cities, urban areas and rural areas, but relies almost entirely on national definitions of these areas. The UN principles and recommendations state that due to different characteristics of urban and rural areas across the globe, a global definition is not possible.

— [[16]](#cite_note-17)

[European](/wiki/Europe "Europe") countries[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*] define urbanized areas on the basis of urban-type [land use](/wiki/Land_use "Land use"), not allowing any gaps of typically more than 200 metres (220 yd), and use [satellite imagery](/wiki/Satellite_imagery "Satellite imagery") instead of census blocks to determine the boundaries of the urban area. In less-developed countries[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*], in addition to land use and density requirements, a requirement that a large majority of the population, typically 75%, is not engaged in agriculture and/or fishing is sometimes used.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Aerial_View_of_Cape_Town_%28iau2305a%29.jpg/330px-Aerial_View_of_Cape_Town_%28iau2305a%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aerial_View_of_Cape_Town_(iau2305a).jpg)

[Cape Town](/wiki/Cape_Town "Cape Town"), South Africa's second-largest metro by population

[South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") has eight metro areas. [Metropolitan municipalities](/wiki/Metropolitan_municipality_(South_Africa) "Metropolitan municipality (South Africa)") (Category A municipalities), as defined in SA, are the most developed areas of the country, and execute all the functions of local government for a [city](/wiki/City "City") or [conurbation](/wiki/Conurbation "Conurbation"). They are created by provincial governments. As per the country's [2022 census](/wiki/2022_South_African_census "2022 South African census") data, South Africa has five [metros](/wiki/Metropolitan_municipality_(South_Africa) "Metropolitan municipality (South Africa)") (major urban areas) with a population of over four million residents, with the other three having a population of around one million. [Metropolitan municipalities](/wiki/Metropolitan_municipality_(South_Africa) "Metropolitan municipality (South Africa)") (Category A municipalities), as defined in SA, are the most developed areas of the country, and execute all the functions of local government for a [city](/wiki/City "City") or [conurbation](/wiki/Conurbation "Conurbation"). They are created by provincial governments.[[17]](#cite_note-18) The country's three branches of government are split over different cities. [Cape Town](/wiki/Cape_Town "Cape Town") is the [legislative](/wiki/Legislature "Legislature") capital, [Pretoria](/wiki/Pretoria "Pretoria") the administrative capital, and [Bloemfontein](/wiki/Bloemfontein "Bloemfontein") the judicial capital. ##### China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: China")]

Main article: [List of cities in China by population](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_China_by_population "List of cities in China by population")

Since 2000, [China](/wiki/China "China")'s cities have expanded at an average rate of 10% annually. It is estimated that China's urban population will increase by 292 million people by 2050,[[4]](#cite_note-UrbanPopulation-5) when its cities will house a combined population of over one billion.[[20]](#cite_note-McKinseyUrbanBillion-21) The country's urbanization rate increased from 17.4% to 46.6% between 1978 and 2009.[[21]](#cite_note-Ref_abcd-22) Between 150 and 200 million [migrant workers](/wiki/Migrant_worker "Migrant worker") work part-time in the major cities, returning home to the countryside periodically with their earnings.[[22]](#cite_note-Harney2008-23)[[23]](#cite_note-Tschang2009-24)

China has more cities with one million or more long-term residents than any other country, including the three [global cities](/wiki/Global_city "Global city") of [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), and [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"); by 2025, the country will be home to 221 cities with over a million inhabitants.[[20]](#cite_note-McKinseyUrbanBillion-21) The figures in the table below are from the 2008 census, and are only estimates of the urban populations within administrative city limits; a different ranking exists when considering the total municipal populations (which includes suburban and rural populations). The large "[floating populations](/wiki/Floating_population "Floating population")" of migrant workers make conducting censuses in urban areas difficult;[[24]](#cite_note-Ref_abce-25) the figures below include only long-term residents.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Japan")]

##### Bangladesh

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Bangladesh")]

In [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), there are total 532 urban areas, which are divided into three categories. Those are [City Corporation](/wiki/List_of_City_Corporations_of_Bangladesh "List of City Corporations of Bangladesh"), [Municipal Corporation](/wiki/List_of_Municipal_Corporations_of_Bangladesh "List of Municipal Corporations of Bangladesh") (Pourasova) and [Upazila town](/wiki/Upazila "Upazila"). Among those urban areas, [Dhaka](/wiki/Dhaka "Dhaka") is the largest city by population and area, with a population of 19.10 million.[[28]](#cite_note-31) In [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), there are total 11 [City Corporations](/wiki/City_Corporations_in_Bangladesh "City Corporations in Bangladesh") and 329 [Municipal Corporations](/wiki/Municipal_Corporations_in_Bangladesh "Municipal Corporations in Bangladesh") and 203 [Small towns](/wiki/Small_town "Small town"), which serves as the center for [Upazilas](/wiki/Upazila "Upazila"). According to 2011 population census, [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") has an urban population of 28%, with a growth rate of 2.8%.[[29]](#cite_note-32) At this growth rate, it is estimated that the urban population of [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") will reach 79 million or 42% of total population by 2035.

##### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: India")]

Main articles: [Urbanization in India](/wiki/Urbanization_in_India "Urbanization in India") and [List of cities in India by population](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_India_by_population "List of cities in India by population")

For the Census of [India](/wiki/India "India") 2011, the definition of urban area is a place having a minimum population of 5,000 of density 400 persons per square kilometre (1,000/sq mi) or higher, and 75% plus of the male working population employed in non-agricultural activities. Places administered by a municipal corporation, cantonment board or notified town area committee are automatically considered urban areas.[[30]](#cite_note-33)

The Census of India 2011 also defined the term "urban agglomeration" as an integrated urban area consisting of a core town together with its "outgrowths" (contiguous suburbs).[[31]](#cite_note-34)

##### Pakistan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Pakistan")]

Main articles: [Urbanisation in Pakistan](/wiki/Urbanisation_in_Pakistan "Urbanisation in Pakistan") and [List of cities in Pakistan by population](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Pakistan_by_population "List of cities in Pakistan by population")

In [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), an area is a major [city](/wiki/City "City") and [municipality](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality") if it has more than 100,000 inhabitants according to census results. Cities include adjacent [cantonments](/wiki/Cantonments_(Pakistan) "Cantonments (Pakistan)").

Urbanisation in [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") has increased since the time of independence and has several different causes. The majority of southern Pakistan's population lives along the [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River"). Karachi is its most populous city.[[34]](#cite_note-37) In the northern half of the country, most of the population lives in an arc formed by the cities of [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore"), [Faisalabad](/wiki/Faisalabad "Faisalabad"), [Rawalpindi](/wiki/Rawalpindi "Rawalpindi"), [Islamabad](/wiki/Islamabad "Islamabad"), [Gujranwala](/wiki/Gujranwala "Gujranwala"), [Sialkot](/wiki/Sialkot "Sialkot"), [Gujrat](/wiki/Gujrat_city "Gujrat city"), [Jhelum](/wiki/Jhelum "Jhelum"), [Sargodha](/wiki/Sargodha "Sargodha"), [Sheikhupura](/wiki/Sheikhupura "Sheikhupura"), [Nowshera](/wiki/Nowshera,_Khyber-Pakhtunkhwa "Nowshera, Khyber-Pakhtunkhwa"), [Mardan](/wiki/Mardan "Mardan") and [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"). During 1990–2008, city dwellers made up 36% of Pakistan's population, making it the most urbanised nation in [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"). Furthermore, 50% of Pakistanis live in towns of 5,000 people or more.[[35]](#cite_note-38) Karachi is the most populated city in [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") closely followed by Lahore according to the [2017 Census](/wiki/2017_Census_of_Pakistan "2017 Census of Pakistan").

#### Southeast Asia

##### Philippines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Philippines")]

In 2020, 54 percent of the [Philippine](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") population lived in urban areas.[[36]](#cite_note-39) With an estimated population of 16.3 million, [Metro Manila](/wiki/Metro_Manila "Metro Manila") is the most populous metropolitan area in the [Philippines](/wiki/List_of_metropolitan_areas_in_the_Philippines "List of metropolitan areas in the Philippines") and the [11th in the world](/wiki/List_of_metropolitan_areas_by_population "List of metropolitan areas by population"). However, the greater urban area is the [5th largest in the world](/wiki/List_of_urban_areas_by_population "List of urban areas by population") with a population of 20,654,307 people (2010 estimate).[[37]](#cite_note-WG-40)

##### Singapore

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Singapore")]

Further information: [Geography of Singapore](/wiki/Geography_of_Singapore "Geography of Singapore") and [Planning areas of Singapore](/wiki/Planning_areas_of_Singapore "Planning areas of Singapore")

As an island [city-state](/wiki/City-state "City-state"), about 5.6 million people live and work within 700 square kilometres (270 sq mi). With 64 [islands and islets](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_Singapore "List of islands of Singapore"), [Singapore Island](/wiki/Singapore_Island "Singapore Island") makes up the largest urban area in the country. According to the [United Nations Economic and Social Commission for Asia and the Pacific](/wiki/United_Nations_Economic_and_Social_Commission_for_Asia_and_the_Pacific "United Nations Economic and Social Commission for Asia and the Pacific"), the country has the highest urbanised population in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"), with 100 percent of its population living in an urban area.[[38]](#cite_note-41) The [Urban Redevelopment Authority](/wiki/Urban_Redevelopment_Authority "Urban Redevelopment Authority") (URA) is responsible for the urban [land-use planning](/wiki/Land-use_planning "Land-use planning"), which designates land use and [urban density](/wiki/Urban_density "Urban density") of the country.[[39]](#cite_note-42) The country is divided into 5 [regions](/wiki/Regions_of_Singapore "Regions of Singapore") for planning purposes by the URA, even though as a city state Singapore is defined as a single continuous urban area. It is further subdivided into 55 [urban planning areas](/wiki/Planning_Areas_of_Singapore "Planning Areas of Singapore"), which acts as the boundaries of [planned towns](/wiki/New_towns_of_Singapore "New towns of Singapore") within the country.[[40]](#cite_note-43)

##### Vietnam

In [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), there are six types of urban areas:

* Special urban area (2 municipalities): [Hanoi](/wiki/Hanoi "Hanoi") and [Ho Chi Minh City](/wiki/Ho_Chi_Minh_City "Ho Chi Minh City").
* Type I urban area (18 provincial cities and 3 municipalities): [Long Xuyên](/wiki/Long_Xuy%C3%AAn "Long Xuyên"), [Pleiku](/wiki/Pleiku "Pleiku"), [Mỹ Tho](/wiki/M%E1%BB%B9_Tho "Mỹ Tho"), [Thủ Dầu Một](/wiki/Th%E1%BB%A7_D%E1%BA%A7u_M%E1%BB%99t "Thủ Dầu Một"), [Bắc Ninh](/wiki/B%E1%BA%AFc_Ninh "Bắc Ninh"), [Biên Hòa](/wiki/Bi%C3%AAn_H%C3%B2a "Biên Hòa"), [Hải Dương](/wiki/H%E1%BA%A3i_D%C6%B0%C6%A1ng "Hải Dương"), [Thanh Hóa](/wiki/Thanh_H%C3%B3a "Thanh Hóa"), [Hạ Long](/wiki/H%E1%BA%A1_Long "Hạ Long"), [Việt Trì](/wiki/Vi%E1%BB%87t_Tr%C3%AC "Việt Trì"), [Thái Nguyên](/wiki/Th%C3%A1i_Nguy%C3%AAn "Thái Nguyên"), [Nam Định](/wiki/Nam_%C4%90%E1%BB%8Bnh "Nam Định"), [Vũng Tàu](/wiki/V%C5%A9ng_T%C3%A0u "Vũng Tàu"), [Buôn Ma Thuột](/wiki/Bu%C3%B4n_Ma_Thu%E1%BB%99t "Buôn Ma Thuột"), [Đà Lạt](/wiki/%C4%90%C3%A0_L%E1%BA%A1t "Đà Lạt"), [Quy Nhơn](/wiki/Quy_Nh%C6%A1n "Quy Nhơn"), [Nha Trang](/wiki/Nha_Trang "Nha Trang"), [Huế](/wiki/Hu%E1%BA%BF "Huế"), [Vinh](/wiki/Vinh "Vinh"), [Cần Thơ](/wiki/C%E1%BA%A7n_Th%C6%A1 "Cần Thơ"), [Đà Nẵng](/wiki/%C4%90%C3%A0_N%E1%BA%B5ng "Đà Nẵng") and [Hải Phòng](/wiki/H%E1%BA%A3i_Ph%C3%B2ng "Hải Phòng").
* Type II urban area (21 provincial cities and 1 district):[Châu Đốc](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2u_%C4%90%E1%BB%91c "Châu Đốc"), [Đồng Hới](/wiki/%C4%90%E1%BB%93ng_H%E1%BB%9Bi "Đồng Hới"), [Uông Bí](/wiki/U%C3%B4ng_B%C3%AD "Uông Bí"), [Bắc Giang](/wiki/B%E1%BA%AFc_Giang "Bắc Giang"), [Ninh Bình](/wiki/Ninh_B%C3%ACnh "Ninh Bình"), [Bạc Liêu](/wiki/B%E1%BA%A1c_Li%C3%AAu "Bạc Liêu"), [Bà Rịa](/wiki/B%C3%A0_R%E1%BB%8Ba "Bà Rịa"), [Thái Bình](/wiki/Th%C3%A1i_B%C3%ACnh "Thái Bình"), [Rạch Giá](/wiki/R%E1%BA%A1ch_Gi%C3%A1 "Rạch Giá"), [Cà Mau](/wiki/C%C3%A0_Mau "Cà Mau"), [Phan Rang–Tháp Chàm](/wiki/Phan_Rang%E2%80%93Th%C3%A1p_Ch%C3%A0m "Phan Rang–Tháp Chàm"), [Tuy Hòa](/wiki/Tuy_H%C3%B2a "Tuy Hòa"), [Phan Thiết](/wiki/Phan_Thi%E1%BA%BFt "Phan Thiết"), [Vĩnh Yên](/wiki/V%C4%A9nh_Y%C3%AAn "Vĩnh Yên"), [Lào Cai](/wiki/L%C3%A0o_Cai "Lào Cai") and [Phú Quốc](/wiki/Ph%C3%BA_Qu%E1%BB%91c "Phú Quốc").

### Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Europe")]

#### Finland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Finland")]

Main articles: [Urban areas in Finland](/wiki/Urban_areas_in_Finland "Urban areas in Finland") and [List of urban areas in Finland by population](/wiki/List_of_urban_areas_in_Finland_by_population "List of urban areas in Finland by population")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Taajama_kyltti_Vimpeliss%C3%A4.jpg/250px-Taajama_kyltti_Vimpeliss%C3%A4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Taajama_kyltti_Vimpeliss%C3%A4.jpg)

A street sign in [Vimpeli](/wiki/Vimpeli "Vimpeli") indicating the beginning of an urban area in Finland

[As in other Nordic countries](/wiki/Urban_areas_in_the_Nordic_countries "Urban areas in the Nordic countries"), an urban area (*taajama* in [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_language "Finnish language")) in Finland must have a building at least every 200 m (660 ft) and at least 200 people. To be considered a [town](/wiki/Town "Town") or a [city](/wiki/City "City") (*kaupunki*) for statistical purposes, an urban area must have at least 15,000 people. This is not to be confused with the city / town designation used by [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Finland "Municipalities of Finland").[[41]](#cite_note-44)[[42]](#cite_note-45)

#### France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: France")]

Main article: [Functional area (France)](/wiki/Functional_area_(France) "Functional area (France)")

In [France](/wiki/France "France"), an urban area (*Fr: [aire d'attraction d'une ville](/wiki/Aire_d%27attraction_d%27une_ville "Aire d'attraction d'une ville")*) is a zone encompassing an area of built-up growth (called an "urban unit" (*[unité urbaine](/wiki/Unit%C3%A9_urbaine "Unité urbaine")*)[[43]](#cite_note-unité_urbaine-46) – close in definition to the North American urban area) and its commuter belt (*[couronne](/wiki/Couronne_p%C3%A9riurbaine "Couronne périurbaine")*). Americans would find the [INSEE](/wiki/INSEE "INSEE") definition of the urban area[[44]](#cite_note-aire_urbaine-47) to be similar to their [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area").

The [largest cities](/wiki/List_of_communes_in_France_with_over_20,000_inhabitants "List of communes in France with over 20,000 inhabitants") in France, in terms of urban area population (2017), are [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") (12,628,266), [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon "Lyon") (2,323,221), [Marseille](/wiki/Marseille "Marseille") (1,760,653), [Toulouse](/wiki/Toulouse "Toulouse") (1,360,829), [Bordeaux](/wiki/Bordeaux "Bordeaux") (1,247,977), [Lille](/wiki/Lille "Lille") (1,191,117), [Nice](/wiki/Nice "Nice") (1,006,201), [Nantes](/wiki/Nantes "Nantes") (972,828), [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg") (790,087) and [Rennes](/wiki/Rennes "Rennes") (733,320).[[45]](#cite_note-48)

Panorama of Paris as seen from the [Eiffel Tower](/wiki/Eiffel_Tower "Eiffel Tower") as full 360-degree view (river flowing from north-east to south-west, right to left

#### Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Germany")]

Main article: [List of cities in Germany by population](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Germany_by_population "List of cities in Germany by population")

[Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") has a number of large [cities](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Germany "List of cities in Germany"). The largest [conurbation](/wiki/Conurbation "Conurbation") is the [Rhine-Ruhr](/wiki/Rhine-Ruhr "Rhine-Ruhr") region (11 million in 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit)), including [Düsseldorf](/wiki/D%C3%BCsseldorf "Düsseldorf") (the capital of [North Rhine-Westphalia](/wiki/North_Rhine-Westphalia "North Rhine-Westphalia")), [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne"), [Bonn](/wiki/Bonn "Bonn"), [Dortmund](/wiki/Dortmund "Dortmund"), [Essen](/wiki/Essen "Essen"), [Duisburg](/wiki/Duisburg "Duisburg"), and [Bochum](/wiki/Bochum "Bochum").[[46]](#cite_note-49)

#### Netherlands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Netherlands")]

The [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") is the [30th-most densely populated](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_population_density "List of countries by population density") country in the world, with 404.6 inhabitants per square kilometre (1,048/sq mi)—or 497 inhabitants per square kilometre (1,287/sq mi) if only the land area is counted. The [Randstad](/wiki/Randstad "Randstad") is the country's largest [conurbation](/wiki/Conurbation "Conurbation") located in the west of the country and contains the four largest cities: Amsterdam, [Rotterdam](/wiki/Rotterdam "Rotterdam"), [The Hague](/wiki/The_Hague "The Hague"), and [Utrecht](/wiki/Utrecht_(city) "Utrecht (city)"). The Randstad has a population of 7 million inhabitants and is the [6th largest](/wiki/List_of_metropolitan_areas_in_Europe_by_population "List of metropolitan areas in Europe by population") [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area") in Europe.

#### Norway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Norway")]

Main article: [List of urban areas in Norway by population](/wiki/List_of_urban_areas_in_Norway_by_population "List of urban areas in Norway by population")

[Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway") defines urban areas ("tettsteder") [similarly to the other Nordic countries](/wiki/Urban_areas_in_the_Nordic_countries "Urban areas in the Nordic countries"). Unlike in Denmark and Sweden, the distance between each building has to be of less than 50 m, although exceptions are made due to parks, industrial areas, rivers, and similar. Groups of houses less than 400 m from the main body of an urban area are included in the urban area.[[47]](#cite_note-ssb1-50)

#### Poland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Poland")]

In [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), official "urban" population figures simply refer to those localities which have the status of towns (*miasta*). The "rural" population is that of all areas outside the boundaries of these towns. This distinction may give a misleading impression in some cases, since some localities with only village status may have acquired larger and denser populations than many many smaller towns[[48]](#cite_note-51) with most excessive example of [Poznań](/wiki/Pozna%C5%84 "Poznań"), most spread urban area of the country with population of the city app. 534 thousand and [metropolitan area](/wiki/Pozna%C5%84_metropolitan_area "Poznań metropolitan area") around 1 million inhabitants. On the other hand, the [Katowice urban area](/wiki/Katowice_urban_area "Katowice urban area") with numerous large and medium cities covers 1,468 km and has above 2 million people.
The [metropolitan areas in Poland](/wiki/Metropolitan_areas_in_Poland "Metropolitan areas in Poland") are the biggest urban zones (e.g. [Katowice metropolitan area](/wiki/Katowice_metropolitan_area "Katowice metropolitan area"), [Łódź metropolitan area](/wiki/%C5%81%C3%B3d%C5%BA_metropolitan_area "Łódź metropolitan area") and [Szczecin metropolitan area](/wiki/Szczecin_metropolitan_area "Szczecin metropolitan area")) and have great impact on the rural surroundings, as it is around [Lublin](/wiki/Lublin "Lublin"), [Radom](/wiki/Radom "Radom"), [Kielce](/wiki/Kielce "Kielce"), [Tarnów](/wiki/Tarn%C3%B3w "Tarnów") and [Białystok](/wiki/Bia%C5%82ystok "Białystok").

#### Russia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Russia")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Business_Centre_of_Moscow_2.jpg/250px-Business_Centre_of_Moscow_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Business_Centre_of_Moscow_2.jpg)

[Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow"), the capital and largest city of [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia")

[Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow"), the capital and [largest city](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Russia_by_population "List of cities and towns in Russia by population") of [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), has a population estimated at 12.4 million residents within the city limits,[[49]](#cite_note-est.2020-52) while over 17 million residents in the urban area,[[50]](#cite_note-Urban-53) and over 20 million residents in the [Moscow Metropolitan Area](/wiki/Moscow_metropolitan_area "Moscow metropolitan area").[[51]](#cite_note-Megapolis-54) It is among the [world's largest cities](/wiki/List_of_largest_cities "List of largest cities"), being the [most populous city](/wiki/List_of_European_cities_by_population_within_city_limits "List of European cities by population within city limits") entirely within [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), the [most populous urban area](/wiki/List_of_urban_areas_in_Europe "List of urban areas in Europe") in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"),[[50]](#cite_note-Urban-53) the [most populous metropolitan area](/wiki/List_of_metropolitan_areas_in_Europe "List of metropolitan areas in Europe") in Europe,[[51]](#cite_note-Megapolis-54) and also the largest city by land area on the European continent.[[52]](#cite_note-Planète_Énergies-55) [Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg "Saint Petersburg"), the cultural capital, is the second-largest city, with a population of roughly 5.4 million inhabitants.[[53]](#cite_note-56) Other major urban areas are [Yekaterinburg](/wiki/Yekaterinburg "Yekaterinburg"), [Novosibirsk](/wiki/Novosibirsk "Novosibirsk"), [Kazan](/wiki/Kazan "Kazan"), [Nizhny Novgorod](/wiki/Nizhny_Novgorod "Nizhny Novgorod"), and [Chelyabinsk](/wiki/Chelyabinsk "Chelyabinsk").

#### Spain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Spain")]

[Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") is a very highly urbanized country. [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid") is its largest urban area. The Southern and Eastern coasts with [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona"), [Valencia](/wiki/Valencia "Valencia") and [Málaga](/wiki/M%C3%A1laga "Málaga") are more urbanised than the Northern and Western ones.

#### Sweden

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Sweden")]

Main article: [List of urban areas in Sweden by population](/wiki/Urban_areas_in_Sweden#Statistics "Urban areas in Sweden")

[Urban areas in Sweden](/wiki/Urban_areas_in_Sweden "Urban areas in Sweden") (*tätorter*) are statistically defined localities, totally independent of the administrative subdivision of the country. There are 1,956 such localities in [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), with a population ranging from 200 to 1,372,000 inhabitants.[[54]](#cite_note-57)

#### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

Main article: [List of urban areas in the United Kingdom](/wiki/List_of_urban_areas_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of urban areas in the United Kingdom")

In 2013 the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom")'s [Office for National Statistics](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics") (ONS) published *2011 Built-up Areas – Methodology and Guidance* which sets out its definition of a **built-up area** (BUA) as an area of built-up land of at least 20 hectares (0.077 sq mi), separated from other settlements by at least 200 metres (660 ft). For 2011 census data there are 5,493 built-up areas, of which 501 are divided into **built-up area sub-divisions** (BUASD) for which data is also available. Each built-up area is named algorithmically, using Ordnance Survey place-name data.[[55]](#cite_note-ons-58)

The ONS has produced census results from urban areas since 1951, since 1981 based upon the extent of irreversible urban development indicated on [Ordnance Survey](/wiki/Ordnance_Survey "Ordnance Survey") maps. The definition is an extent of at least 20 ha and at least 1,500 census residents. Separate areas are linked if less than 200 m (220 yd) apart. Included are transportation features.[[56]](#cite_note-59) The UK has five Urban Areas with a population over a million and a further sixty nine with a [population over one hundred thousand](/wiki/List_of_urban_areas_in_the_United_Kingdom#Most_populous "List of urban areas in the United Kingdom").

### North America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: North America")]

#### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Canada")]

Main article: [List of the largest population centres in Canada](/wiki/List_of_the_largest_population_centres_in_Canada "List of the largest population centres in Canada")

According to [Statistics Canada](/wiki/Statistics_Canada "Statistics Canada"), an urban area in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") is an area with a population of at least 1,000 people where the density is no fewer than 400 persons per square kilometre (1,000/sq mi).[[60]](#cite_note-StatCan1-63) If two or more urban areas are within 2 km (1.2 mi) of each other by road, they are merged into a single urban area, provided they do not cross [census metropolitan area](/wiki/Census_metropolitan_area "Census metropolitan area") or [census agglomeration](/wiki/Census_agglomeration "Census agglomeration") boundaries.[[61]](#cite_note-StatCan2-64)

In the [Canada 2011 Census](/wiki/Canada_2011_Census "Canada 2011 Census"), Statistics Canada redesignated urban areas with the new term "[population centre](/wiki/Population_centre_(Canada) "Population centre (Canada)")";[[62]](#cite_note-popcentre-65) the new term was chosen in order to better reflect the fact that urban vs. rural is not a strict division, but rather a continuum within which several distinct settlement patterns may exist. For example, a community may fit a strictly statistical definition of an urban area, but may not be commonly thought of as "urban" because it has a smaller population, or functions socially and economically as a suburb of another urban area rather than as a self-contained urban entity, or is geographically remote from other urban communities. Accordingly, the new definition set out three distinct types of population centres: small (population 1,000 to 29,999), medium (population 30,000 to 99,999) and large (population 100,000 or greater).[[62]](#cite_note-popcentre-65) Despite the change in terminology, however, the demographic definition of a population centre remains unchanged from that of an urban area: a population of at least 1,000 people where the density is no fewer than 400 persons per km2.

#### Mexico

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Mexico")]

[Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") is one of many countries where the urbanization rate is at least 80%. [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City"), its capital, is the largest urban area in the country.

#### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: United States")]

Main article: [List of United States urban areas](/wiki/List_of_United_States_urban_areas "List of United States urban areas")

In the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), the [Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") defines urban areas and delineates urban area boundaries after each census. The Bureau defines an urban area as "a statistical geographic entity consisting of a densely settled core created from [census blocks](/wiki/Census_blocks "Census blocks") and contiguous qualifying territory that together have at least 2,000 housing units or 5,000 persons."[[63]](#cite_note-US_2020_criteria-66) There were 2,646 urban areas identified by the Census Bureau for 2020. 511 of these had a population of 50,000 or more.[[64]](#cite_note-US_2020_list-67)

For the 2000 and 2010 censuses, the Census Bureau differentiated between two kinds of urban areas: urbanized areas and urban clusters. The term *urbanized area* denoted an urban area of 50,000 or more people. Urban areas under 50,000 people were called *urban clusters*. Urbanized areas were first delineated in the United States in the 1950 census, while urban clusters were added in the 2000 census. The distinction between urbanized areas and urban clusters was removed for the 2020 census.[[63]](#cite_note-US_2020_criteria-66)

Urban areas consist of a densely-settled urban core, plus surrounding developed areas that meet certain density criteria. Since urban areas are composed of census blocks and not cities, counties, or county-equivalents, urban area boundaries may consist of partial areas of these political units. Urban areas are distinguished from [rural areas](/wiki/Rural_areas "Rural areas"): any area not part of an urban area is considered to be rural by the Census Bureau.[[63]](#cite_note-US_2020_criteria-66)

The largest urban area in the United States is that of [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") and its surrounding suburbs. The New York–[Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey")–[Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"), NY–NJ urban area had a population of 19,426,449 as of 2020, while the larger [metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area") had a population of 20,140,470, and the [combined statistical area](/wiki/Combined_statistical_area "Combined statistical area") had a population of 23,582,649. The next five largest urban areas in the U.S. are those of [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), [Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami"), [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston"), and [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas").[[64]](#cite_note-US_2020_list-67) 80.0 percent of the population of the United States lives within the boundaries of an urban area as of the 2020 census.[[65]](#cite_note-2020_press_release-68)

The concept of Urbanized Areas as defined by the U.S. Census Bureau is often used as a more accurate gauge of the size of a city, since in different cities and states the lines between city borders and the urbanized area of that city are often not the same. For example, the city of [Greenville, South Carolina](/wiki/Greenville,_South_Carolina "Greenville, South Carolina") has a city population just over 68,000 and an urbanized area population of around 400,000, while [Greensboro, North Carolina](/wiki/Greensboro,_North_Carolina "Greensboro, North Carolina") has a city population just over 285,000 and an urbanized area population of around 300,000 — meaning that Greenville is actually "larger" for some intents and purposes, but not for others, such as taxation, local elections, etc.

In the [U.S. Department of Agriculture](/wiki/U.S._Department_of_Agriculture "U.S. Department of Agriculture")'s [natural resources inventory](/wiki/Natural_resources_inventory "Natural resources inventory"), urban areas are officially known as *developed areas* or urban and built-up areas. Such areas include cities, ethnic villages, other built-up areas of more than 10 ac (4 ha), industrial sites, railroad yards, cemeteries, airports, golf courses, shooting ranges, institutional and public administration sites, and similar areas. The 1997 national resources inventory placed over 98,000,000 ac (40,000,000 ha) in this category, an increase of 25,000,000 ac (10,000,000 ha) since 1982.[[66]](#cite_note-69)

### Oceania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Oceania")]

#### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Australia")]

The [Australian Bureau of Statistics](/wiki/Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics "Australian Bureau of Statistics") refers to urban areas as Urban Centres, which it generally defines as population clusters of 1,000 or more people.[[67]](#cite_note-AUS-70) Australia is one of the most urbanised countries in the world, with more than 50% of the population residing in Australia's three biggest urban centres.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[67]](#cite_note-AUS-70)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Sydney_Harbour_Bridge_night.jpg/960px-Sydney_Harbour_Bridge_night.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sydney_Harbour_Bridge_night.jpg "Sydney is Australia's largest city, home to 5.3 million inhabitants.")

[Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney") is Australia's largest city, home to 5.3 million inhabitants.[[68]](#cite_note-ABSMRSYD-71)

#### New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: New Zealand")]

Main article: [Urban areas of New Zealand](/wiki/Urban_areas_of_New_Zealand "Urban areas of New Zealand")

[Statistics New Zealand](/wiki/Statistics_New_Zealand "Statistics New Zealand") defines urban areas in New Zealand, which are independent of any administrative subdivisions and have no legal basis.[[69]](#cite_note-defn-72) There are four classes of urban area: major urban areas (population 100,000+), large urban areas (population 30,000–99,999), medium urban areas (population 10,000–29,999) and small urban areas (population 1,000–9,999). As of 2021, there are 7 major urban areas, 13 large urban areas, 22 medium urban areas and 136 small urban areas. Urban areas are reclassified after each [New Zealand census](/wiki/New_Zealand_census "New Zealand census"), so population changes between censuses does not change an urban area's classification.

### South America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: South America")]

#### Argentina

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Argentina")]

Argentina is highly urbanized.[[71]](#cite_note-74) The ten largest metropolitan areas account for half of the population, and fewer than one in ten live in rural areas. About 3 million people live in Buenos Aires City and the [Greater Buenos Aires](/wiki/Greater_Buenos_Aires "Greater Buenos Aires") metropolitan area totals around 15 million, making it one of the largest urban areas in the world, with a population of 18 million all up.[[72]](#cite_note-majorcities-75)

[Córdoba](/wiki/C%C3%B3rdoba,_Argentina "Córdoba, Argentina") has around 1.5 million people living in the urban area, while [Rosario](/wiki/Rosario "Rosario"), [Mendoza](/wiki/Mendoza,_Argentina "Mendoza, Argentina") and [Tucumán](/wiki/San_Miguel_de_Tucum%C3%A1n "San Miguel de Tucumán") have around 1.2 million inhabitants each[[72]](#cite_note-majorcities-75) and [La Plata](/wiki/La_Plata "La Plata"), [Mar del Plata](/wiki/Mar_del_Plata "Mar del Plata"), [Salta](/wiki/Salta "Salta") and [Santa Fe](/wiki/Santa_Fe,_Argentina "Santa Fe, Argentina")[[72]](#cite_note-majorcities-75)[[73]](#cite_note-76) have at least 500,000 people each.

#### Brazil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Brazil")]

The following paragraph is an excerpt from [List of metropolitan areas in Brazil § Definitions](/wiki/List_of_metropolitan_areas_in_Brazil#Definitions "List of metropolitan areas in Brazil").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=List_of_metropolitan_areas_in_Brazil&action=edit)]

In Brazil, the terms *[metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area")* ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *região metropolitana*) and *[urban agglomeration](/wiki/Urban_agglomeration "Urban agglomeration")* (*aglomeração urbana*) have specific meanings. They are defined by federal and state legislation as collections of [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Brazil "Municipalities of Brazil") focused on "integrating the organization, planning and execution of public functions of common interest".[[74]](#cite_note-77) An *integrated development area* (*região integrada de desenvolvimento*) is one of the two above structures that crosses state (or [Federal District](/wiki/Federal_District_(Brazil) "Federal District (Brazil)")) boundaries.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Urban_area&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Chile")]
